coredata.dc:title: Polyvinyl alcohol and silver decorated carbon quantum-dots for new nano-composites with application electromagnetic interface (EMI) shielding
coredata.dc:creator: El-Shamy, Ahmed Gamal
coredata.dc:description: In this article, the marriage of (Ag@CDots) and PVA has been done via the solution casting approach for the electromagnetic shielding performance. Decoration of Ag on the CDots surface is performed by precipitation method in order to increase the conductivity of the nano-composite. The synergetic effect of nanofillers on the morphology, structure, mechanical, dielectric and electrical properties has been extensively studied. SEM analysis has displayed a high dispersion and an excellent adhesion of Ag@CDots in PVA template. The conductivity tends to increase, when Ag@CDots increased, to be 31.95 S/m at 8 wt.% of Ag@CDots due to formation of conductive path of Ag@CDots. Also, the dielectric constant increases with increasing Ag@CDots nano-particles due to formation of microcapacitors in the template of nano-composite. Young's modulus has dramatically increased up to 9.1 ± 0.71 GPa for 8 wt.% of Ag@CDots. Ag@CDots/PVA nano-composite displays the total shielding effectiveness (SET) of electromagnetic interface (EMI) of 56 dB at 8 GHz at thickness 1 mm, due to the equal contribution of EM reflection and absorption in the main mechanism of shielding effectiveness. Finally, the fine dispersion and the brilliant features harmonize together to make (Ag@CDots/PVA) nano-composites having a high total value of shielding (SET) and producing a shielding in X-band range about 99.9 % of electromagnetic radiation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PVA
CQDs nano-particles
Ag nano-particles
Electrical conductivity
Electromagnetic shielding

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of (Ag@CDots) and their (Ag@CDots/PVA) nano-composite films
Structural and Mechanical properties of Ag@CDots/PVA nano-composite films
EMI shielding studies of Ag@CDots/PVA nano-composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a), Shows the full XPS spectra of the Ag@CDots nanoparticles. (b) shows the XPS spectra of the carbon (C1s). (c) shows the XPS of the Oxygen (O1s). (d) shows the XPS spectra of the silver (Ag3d). (e) XRD patterns of Ag@CDots nanoparticles (f) FTIR spectra of CDots and Ag@CDots nanoparticles (g) TEM and HRTEM images of Ag@CDots nanoparticles.
XRD patterns of the Ag@CDots/PVA nanocomposite films with different concentration of Ag@CDots nanoparticles.

Surface morphology (SEM micrograph) of the (Ag@CDots/PVA) nanocomposite films with different Ag@CDots concentration (a) 2 wt.%. (b) 4 wt.%. (c) 6 wt.%. (d) 8 wt.%. (e) shows the EDS spectra of the (Ag@CDots/PVA) nanocomposite films with 8 wt.% Ag@CDots, which shows the signals of Carbon, Oxygen, and Silver elements and (f) TEM micrograph of the Ag@CDots/PVA nanocomposite film (Ag@CDots =8 wt.%), (The sample prepared by taking a thin layer of the film by using ultra-microtome technique).

(a) The mechanical properties (Stress-Strain behavior) of the Ag@CDots/PVA nanocomposite films with different concentration of Ag@CDots nanoparticles. The mechanical parameters (b) Young modulus (c) Tensile strength (d) Elongation at break of the Ag@CDots/PVA nanocomposite films with different concentration of Ag@CDots nanoparticles.

(a) The electrical conductivity as function of Ag@CDots concentration for the Ag@CDots/PVA nanocomposite films. (b) The electrical conductivity of 8 wt.% Ag/PVA, 8 wt.% CDots/PVA and 8 wt.% Ag@CDots /PVA nanocomposite films, for comparison.
(a) Dielectric constant of the Ag@CDots/PVA nanocomposites versus frequency, and (b) Dielectric constant of 8 wt.% Ag/PVA, 8 wt.% CDots/PVA and 8 wt.% Ag@CDots/PVA nanocomposites, for comparison.
(a) Dielectric loss (b) Tan (δ) of the Ag@CDots/PVA nanocomposites versus frequency at different Ag@CDots concentrations.

(a) The experimental shielding effectiveness values of the Ag@CDots/PVA nanocomposite films as a function of frequency in the (X-band) range. (b) Scheme shows the mechanism of the transmission, internal reflection and absorption of electromagnetic wave with the nanocomposite films. (c) The experimental shielding effectiveness of 8 wt.% Ag/PVA, 8 wt.% CDots/PVA and 8 wt.% Ag@CDots/PVA nanocomposites, for comparison.

(a) SEA and (b) SER of the Ag@CDots/PVA nanocomposites versus frequency in the (X-band) range at different concentration of Ag@CDots nanoparticles, (c) SEA of the 8 wt.% Ag/PVA, 8 wt.% CDots/PVA and 8 wt.% Ag@CDots/PVA films, for comparison. (d) SER of the 8 wt.% Ag/PVA, 8 wt.% CDots/PVA and 8 wt.% Ag@CDots/PVA films, for comparison.
Fabrication scenario of Ag@CDots/PVA nanocomposite for electromagnetic wave shielding.
Microwave shielding values of various polymer nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: Polyvinyl alcohol and silver decorated carbon quantum-dots for new nano-composites with application electromagnetic interface (EMI) shielding

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Suez Canal University, Ismailia, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Suez Canal University, Ismailia, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, the marriage of (Ag@CDots) and PVA has been done via the solution casting approach for the electromagnetic shielding performance. Decoration of Ag on the CDots surface is performed by precipitation method in order to increase the conductivity of the nano-composite. The synergetic effect of nanofillers on the morphology, structure, mechanical, dielectric and electrical properties has been extensively studied. SEM analysis has displayed a high dispersion and an excellent adhesion of Ag@CDots in PVA template. The conductivity tends to increase, when Ag@CDots increased, to be 31.95 S/m at 8 wt.% of Ag@CDots due to formation of conductive path of Ag@CDots. Also, the dielectric constant increases with increasing Ag@CDots nano-particles due to formation of microcapacitors in the template of nano-composite. Young's modulus has dramatically increased up to 9.1 ± 0.71 GPa for 8 wt.% of Ag@CDots. Ag@CDots/PVA nano-composite displays the total shielding effectiveness (SET) of electromagnetic interface (EMI) of 56 dB at 8 GHz at thickness 1 mm, due to the equal contribution of EM reflection and absorption in the main mechanism of shielding effectiveness. Finally, the fine dispersion and the brilliant features harmonize together to make (Ag@CDots/PVA) nano-composites having a high total value of shielding (SET) and producing a shielding in X-band range about 99.9 % of electromagnetic radiation.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, environmental pollution represented in the emission of electromagnetic radiations from electronic and wireless devices has been drawing the attention of researchers and scientists, due to their potential and hazardous effects on human health (by causing cancer, brain tumor, breaking down DNA strands and etc.) when humans expose to electromagnetic fields for a long-standing time [1–4]. So, the quality and assurance of life in modern societies are measured by protecting humans from the exposure to electromagnetic energy and it is considered as a primary hygienic requirement [5,6]. In military, electronic, and electrical commercial applications, the requirements of blocking and inhibiting the electromagnetic waves particularly in microwave frequency are extremely important [3,4].

For a long time, metals and alloys were perfect choices for electromagnetic (SE) shielding, until many shortcomings were reported for these materials such as cost inefficiency, heaviness, inflexibility, instability to environmental conditions (oxidize or react with some chemicals), hard formation in complex shapes/hard shaping, and so forth [7,8]. So, with the rapid growth and proliferation of wireless and electronic industry, new features were provided such as lighter weight, flexibility, affordability, productability and excellent environmental resistance [9–12]. In addition, there are inevitable demands for high electrically conductive materials to inhibit electromagnetic waves to satisfy the requirements of medical and electric applications as well as military industries [8,9]. In response to these urgent needs, many highly conductive materials have been introduced with high shielding performance and lightweight innovation for example, polymer nano-composites based on carbon materials such as CNTs, carbon blacks, fullerene, GO and graphene to replace metallic fillers [13–15]. Carbon materials have been frequently applied to provide multi-functions such as anti-static and EMI shielding, as well as for the construction of capacitors and batteries [1–4]. Having an amazing nano-structure, peculiar mechanical parameters, and high electrical and thermal conductivities, these materials can enhance different properties of polymers once they are embedded on it [5–8]. Doping polymeric materials with carbon nano-structures gives the desired electrical properties and electromagnetic shielding performance at very low filler concentrations.

In spite of, the extensive usage of carbon materials with different shapes (CNTs, GO and others) in many applications, they still have some shortcomings when they are embedded into the polymer such as the deterioration/weakening of the physical properties of the produced nano-composites due to agglomeration, aggregation processes and non-uniform dispersion of fillers that prevent them from being used a large scale production [16]. The agglomeration and aggregation are produced from the higher volume fraction and higher surface area of these materials [17,18]. Many literatures reported that the high aspect ratio and the low bending stiffness make these materials easy to aggregate in the matrix of polymer [19,20]. Pan and Bian [21] reported on the impact of agglomeration of CNT on the mechanical features of composites and they observed that CNTs have negative side effects on the mechanical parameters owing to the agglomeration of CNT. Also, Deep and Mishra [22] reported the decline in the mechanical parameters such as (strength, impact strength and others) with the growth of CNT on the matrix of poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) nano-composite, due to the poor interfacial adhesion behavior between PMMA and CNT.

In pursuit of overcoming the above limitations, the lower mechanical, electrical and electromagnetic shielding performance limitations, we introduce a new nano-composite based on silver (Ag) decorated carbon dots (CDots) embedded in poly vinyl alcohol (PVA), (Ag@CDots/PVA) nano-composite, to achieve highly flexible, highly conductive and excellent electromagnetic shielding performance materials. In fact, polyvinyl alcohol polymer (PVA) is a fascinating material due to its superior applications and features such as its stability against environmental conditions, processability, and others [1]. Also, carbon dots (CDots) as zero-dimensional nano-particles have seemed to be unique materials owing to their special features for instance high electrical and thermal conductivity, high thermoelectric behavior, photostability, bio-compatibility, high water solubility, excellent luminescence behavior, and harmlessness [17,23–31]. More importantly, their optical behavior such as tunable PL spectra extended from UV to (NIR) light and their up-conversion behavior depending on CDots size, shape, groups functionalized CDots surfaces and embedding materials [17,23–31]. Furthermore, silver (Ag) was inserted into diverse polymeric materials with crystalline or amorphous nature to increase its electrical, mechanical and other properties due to the high conducting behavior of silver [17,23–31]. Recently, Al-Ghamdi et al. [32] used the chemical reduction approach to synthesize (Ag/PVA) nano-composite system for electromagnetic shielding. It was found that SET was 36 dB (for 8 wt.% of Ag nano-particles) at 1 GHz. Also, they found that the young modulus (Ymodulus) was ∼ 45 MPa at the same Ag content. Moreover, El-Shamy [31] prepared the flexible system (CDots/PVA) nano-composite for electromagnetic shielding properties. It was found that SET is around 37 dB for 8 wt.% of CDots at 1 GHz. Also, it was reported that the young modulus was found to be 4.48 GPa at the same concentration of CDots in PVA.

The SET of the polymer nano-composites is obtained either by EM absorption or EM reflection depending on the doping nano-particles and their dispersion and distribution in the polymer matrix [33–35]. So, the shielding materials should have high EM absorption or reflection to obtain high EMI shielding effectiveness behavior.

So as to get higher (SET) shielding effectiveness, Ag nano-particles decorated CDots are used, so that the synergetic effect of the two (CDots and Ag) nano-particles can improve/increase the shielding effectiveness of the shielding materials.

Indeed, no literatures were reported on the electromagnetic shielding properties of (Ag@CDots/PVA) films until now, according to the best of our survey. In fact, PVA polymer was selected here due to its high dielectric properties, good permeability, adhesive property, capability of binding, easy processability, its high flexibility and mechanical properties, while Ag@CDots nano-particles were significantly used as fillers to increase the electrical conductivity and their synergetic effect in order to increase the EM reflection, EM absorption and the electrical conductivity and hence EM shielding performance. In this issue, we report an inexpensive and easy synthesis of (Ag@CDots/PVA) nano-composite by using casting techniques for the electromagnetic shielding properties. Decoration of Ag nano-particles on the surface of CDots was done by precipitation method, then embedding them into PVA matrix. In addition, the work aims at discovering the synergetic effect of Ag@CDots on electrical and dielectric properties of Ag@CDots/PVA composite, and they were found to be tuned with the Ag@CDots nano-particles concentration. Electrical conductivity of the composite increases with growing the Ag@CDots concentration and its higher value was 31.95 S/m at 8 wt.% of Ag@CDots nano-particles, due to formation of Ag@CDots conductive paths in the nano-composite. In addition, the values of dielectric constant increase with increasing Ag@CDots concentration due to creation of the microcapacitors in the composite. Moreover, the effect of Ag@CDots on the electromagnetic shielding performance is studied. It was found that the total shielding effectiveness value was 56 dB at 8 wt.% of Ag@CDots nano-particles. The electromagnetic reflection and absorption are equally contributed in the main mechanism of high EMI shielding effectiveness. Also, it was found that the Young's modulus increased dramatically to be 9.1 ± 0.71 GPa for 8 wt.% of Ag@CDots as compared to 0.24 ± 0.22 GPa for fresh PVA. Finally, it is recommended that Ag@CDots/PVA materials may find potential applications in catalysis reactors, wearable devices, biological and electromagnetic shielding materials.

Poly(vinyl alcohol) (M w = 104 g/mole), silver nitrate (AgNO3) and Sodium Borohydride (NaBH4) were purchased from the Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals. d-glucose (99.8 %) and acetone (99.9 %) were purchased from Al-Nasr Chemicals Company, Egypt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of (Ag@CDots) and their (Ag@CDots/PVA) nano-composite films
ce:section.ce:para: At first, it is worth to mention that, PVA, Ag/PVA, CDots/PVA nano-composites have been prepared by using the casting technique [17,23–31], see supplementary materials S1–S3.

Precipitation method was used to decorate CDots with Ag nano-particles by preparing an aqueous solution of 155 mg AgNO3 in 100 mL of CDots solution. Then, 1 M of NaBH4 solution was inserted into the above (CDots-AgNO3) solution by drop/second and the obtained mixture was agitated for 1 h to reduce Ag+ ions to Ag atoms that is attached to CDots nano-particle surface through the reduction reaction. Then, aqueous solution of NaOH (5 M) was added dropwise until reaching the pH value 11. Then, the reaction was left for 3 h, followed by washing the produced Ag@CDots nano-particles with water to eliminate the excess of the hydroxyl ions (OH−) and nitrate ions (NO3 +), then the obtained Ag@CDots nano-particles dried at 40 °C for 10 h.

Casting technique has been used to fabricate the nano-composite (Ag@CDots/PVA) films [17,23–31], see Scheme 1 ). The fabrication of the films was performed as follow: an aqueous solution of (Ag@CDots) prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of Ag@CDots in 10 mL of deionized water to make the desired concentrations from 2 to 8 wt.%. Then this solution was added dropwise to PVA solution, prepared by dissolving 2 g of PVA into 50 mL of deionized water. The obtained mixture was casted and left in an electric oven for 24 h to evaporate water. By using the digital micrometer, the film thickness was measured and it was found to be 220 ± 0.2 μm. The films were divided into strips and labeled PCQG0, PCQG2, PCQG4, PCQG6 and PCQG8 for Ag@CDots concentration 0, 2, 4, 6 and 8 wt.%, respectively.

X-ray (Brucker Axs-D8) diffractometer was applied to study the structure of (Ag@CDots/PVA) samples. The elemental compositions of the nano-particles (Ag@CDots) have been detected by using XPS (AXIS ULTRA, Kratos Analytical apparatus). By using KBr pellet method, the FTIR spectra of Ag@CDots were measured from 4000 to 400 cm−1 by a Nicolet DTGS TEC instrument. The electrical conductivity of the films (with thickness d and resistance R) was measured by using (Keithley 6517B) electrometer by covering both film sides with Ag paste on the desired area (A). The equation
σ =
d
R A
(S/m) was applied to determine the DC conductivity. To study the size and shape of the nano-particles inside the films, (JEOL-JEM-1230) TEM microscope was applied. TEM measurement for films was carried out by cutting a thin layer from the said films by using a beam of laser (ultra-microtome technique). The Ag@CDots/PVA surface morphology was analyzed by applying the (JEOL JSM- 6500 F) SEM microscope, which is attached with an EDS unit, using an electron beam with a current of 40 nA and energy 15 keV. A thin layer of gold was covered the film surfaces by using a vacuum evaporator to make the films conductive for SEM measurements. RLC Bridge (3531 Z-Hitester, Hioke, Japan) was used to measure the dielectric parameters of the nano-composites at frequencies of 42 Hz – 1 MHz by coating the two faces of the film with silver layer as electrodes. By using the waveguide line (Hewlett–Packard) instrument with a spectro-analyzer, the (SE) of Ag@CDots/PVA films was done. A disk shape with special dimensions (Ddiameter = 2.06 cm and thickness =1 mm) of the (Ag@CDots/PVA) films was prepared for the SE measurements [31]. The special films have been prepared for SE measurements by controlling the area of petri-dishes to control the thickness of the films.

At first, the element compositions of Ag@CDots nano-particles were analyzed. The full spectrum of (XPS) shows that Ag@CDots nano-particle has three elements C, O, and Ag, see (Fig. 1 a). A peak for C1s was identified at Ebinding = 288 eV, and a peak for O1s was detected at Ebinding =535 eV. The other detected peak for Ag element at Ebinding =535 eV. From the (Fig. 1b), the spectrum of C1s element showed four sharp peaks at Ebinding = 283.8 eV, 285.5 eV, 287.3 eV and 284.7 eV for the CC (sp2 type of carbon hybridization), the OC or OC (sp2 type of carbon hybridization), the COOH groups and COH groups, respectively [31,33]. The presence of these functionalized groups demonstrates the graphite structure of CDots. (Fig. 1c) shows the spectrum of O1s, it can be noticed that there is a peak at 531.2 eV for the OC groups [31,34], and peak at Ebinding =532.4 eV for the OC groups. Moreover, another peak at 533.4 eV for the OHC and/or OHC = O groups. The spectrum of Ag3d shows two peaks at Ebinding =367 eV and 373.5 eV for the Ag(3d5/2) and Ag(3d3/2), respectively, (Fig. 1d).

Also, XRD patterns of Ag@CDots nano-particles were carried out to confirm the formation and decoration of Ag on the surface of CDots (Fig. 1e). Four diffraction patterns were detected at 2θ = 38.5°, 44.5°, 64.7° and 77.5° corresponding to the (hkl) planes (111), (200), (220) and (311) of the FCC structure of Ag nano-particles [17,23–31]. Besides these patterns, an additional pattern was detected at 2θ = 23° for C(002) plane of hexagonal lattice structure of CDots [17,23–31]. More evidence was done by measuring FTIR for CDots and Ag@CDots (Fig. 1f). FTIR spectra of CDots display a band at 1641 cm−1, this band is assigned to CC vibrations, which is the core unit of CDots. The peak at 3392 cm−1 is attributed to the OH vibration. Also, the peaks at 1026 and 1076 cm−1 are ascribed to the CO in CDots. Two bands at 1415 and 2929 cm−1 are ascribed to the CH vibration [17,23–31]. When Ag decorated CDots, the position of these peaks was shifted indicating the interaction and decoration of Ag on CDots surface [36].

Next, TEM and HRTEM micrographs were performed to clarify the location and distribution of the components in Ag@CDots particles (Fig. 1g). It is clear that, tiny Ag particles decorating the surface of CDots. CDots were observed with a spherical shape and their size was found ∼ 15 nm. HRTEM image shows the crystalline structure of plane (110) of sp2 graphitic CDots with 0.21 nm lattice space [36], and (111) plane of Ag nano-particles with 0.24 nm lattice space [17,23–31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and Mechanical properties of Ag@CDots/PVA nano-composite films
ce:section.ce:para:
XRD patterns for (Ag@CDots/PVA) films were performed at room temperature. It is known that polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is a semi-crystalline polymer. The crystalline part in PVA was observed at 2θ = 19.5° as shown in XRD chart, see Fig. 2 . The hydrogen H-bonding (intra and intermolecular type) between the segments is accountable for the crystallinity of PVA [17,23–31]. When CDots nano-particles are inserted into PVA, the crystallinity of PVA increases due to growth of H-bonding number between the functionalized (−OH) groups on CDots surface and (−OH) groups in PVA, see our previous work [17,23–31]. Also, when (Ag@CDots) nano-particles inserted into PVA, the increase in PVA crystallinity was observed owing to growth of number of hydrogen H-bonding in (Ag@CDots/PVA) nano-composites. This is due to when Ag decorates CDots surface, both the CDots and Ag interact with PVA through (−OH groups) [37,38], this increases number of H-bonding between Ag@CDots and PVA, thus increasing the crystallinity of the nano-composite. In addition to this peak, there are four additional peaks for Ag nano-particles at 2θ = 38.5°, 44.5°, 64.7° and 77.5° representing the diffraction peaks of the planes (111), (200), (220) and (311) for the face centered cube system of the Ag nano-particles [17,23–31]. Also, the degree of crystallinity of the nanocomposite films was calculated [17,23–31] from the relation of the integrated area under the crystalline regions divided by the summation of both the integrated areas of the crystalline and amorphous regions according to the relation;
χ =
A c
/ (
A c
+
A a
)
, where Ac is the integrated area under the crystalline part and Aa is the integrated area under the amorphous part. The crystallinity of the films was determined, and it was found 39.56 ± 2 %, 42.22 ± 1 %, 45.87 ± 1%, 48.91 ± 1 % and 52 ± 1 % for PCQG0, PCQG2, PCQG4, PCQG6 and PCQG8, respectively.

SEM micrograph was performed for studying the surface morphology of Ag@CDots/PVA films, see Fig. 3 . As seen from the figure, Ag@CDots nano-particles are well distributed with a high degree of homogeneity and also they have a fine dispersion in PVA template. On the other hand, Ag@CDots nano-particles show some kinds of slightly aggregation and agglomeration, also having a spherical shaping on the surface of the film thanks to Ag@CDots nano-particles assembling in the films during the evaporation of solvent; see (Fig. 3a–d). A better and fine dispersion of Ag@CDots in the matrix of PVA with some kinds of slightly agglomeration and aggregation of Ag@CDots in PVA was observed by increasing CDots concentration, because of the strong H-bonding interaction between Ag@CDots and PVA [17,23–31]. This produces a percolating network of Ag@CDots nano-particles that is responsible for the creation of spherical structures and hence enhancement the mechanical properties. SEM micrograph for (PCQG8) sample shows a fine distribution and high adhesion property between Ag@CDots and PVA polymer in the whole matrix of the polymer [31,35]. Also, it can be seen that a high packing density for (PCQG8) sample, enhancement of the inter-particle connectivity (like particle-to-particle behavior) and the aspect ratio parameter was enhanced [27]. These factors all together are playing an important role in increasing the conductivity (
σ
e l e c t r i c a l
) , mechanical behavior and high EMI shielding [28]. Also, EDS spectra for the sample PCQG8 were performed to identify the elements in the films (Fig. 3e). As seen, the nano-composites have a spectrum at energy 0.2 KeV, this is due to the carbon element from both CDots and PVA. Also, a spectrum at energy 0.5 KeV for oxygen is coming from both CDots and PVA. In addition to, a spectrum at energy 3.4 keV was detected for Ag nano-particles [17,23–31].

TEM analysis for a thin layer of the nano-composite was performed by using the ultra-microtome method, (Fig. 3f). It is clear that Ag@CDots particles are in nano-scale and their average particle size is ∼15 nm. Also, Ag@CDots nano-particles have a spherical shape with a fine distribution in PVA matrix. Moreover, they are stacked uniformly. This way of arrangement makes a path of conductive Ag@CDots nano-particles, which facilitates the increase in the conductivity of the samples. Also, Ag@CDots nanoparticles are separated from each other with a thin layer of PVA polymer. This layer serves as a micro-capacitor which accumulates the electrical charges and increases the dielectric value.

The stress-strain behavior of (Ag@CDots/PVA) films was done (Fig. 4 a). Young’s modulus (Ymodulus), tensile strength (Tstrength) and the elongation at break were plotted versus the concentration of Ag@CDots (Fig. 4b–d). The Young’s modulus (Ymodulus) and tensile strength (Tstrength) were increased and the elongation at break was decreased with increasing the concentration of Ag@CDots. This is may be owing to the increase in the packing density of Ag@CDots in the nano-composites [17,23–31]. This packing density was increased when Ag@CDots concentration increased producing a decrease in the lattice strain under the applied stress. The increase in the packing density leads to blocking PVA macro-molecules from sliding over each other, this behavior produced by H-bonding (inter/intra type), leads to increasing the charge transfer complex inside the composite, which in turns increases the Young’s modulus (Ymodulus), tensile strength (Tstrength) and conductivity. Also, we observed that, with increasing the concentration of Ag@CDots the elongation at break declines (Fig. 4d). The decrease in the elongation at break or the plastic region may be attributed to (i) the increase in the crosslinking density (intra-intermolecular H-bonding type). The higher cross-linking density, the smaller possibility of orientation, increasing the strength increase the stiffness of the polymer [17,23–31]. So, the increasing of the cross-linking leads to the decrease in elongation at break. Where the increase in the cross-linking leads to increasing the thermal stability of the nano-composites and makes them insoluble in an organic solvent property. (ii) The decrease in the polymer chain unravel, and these chains aligning perpendicular to the direction of the applied stress causing the decrease in the elongation at break (plastic region).

The dc conductivity of Ag@CDots/PVA nano-composite against the concentration of Ag@CDots nano-particle was studied (Fig. 5 a). It is obvious that with increasing the Ag@CDots concentration, the dc conductivity of Ag@CDots/PVA nano-composite increases. This is attributed to the creation of a conductive path from Ag@CDots nano-particles in PVA template [17,23–31]. This conductive path facilitates the generation of a charge transfer complexs between PVA molecular chains and Ag@CDots nano-particle. This leads to increasing the charge carrier mobility in the nano-composite and hence increases the conductivity of the nano-composite.

In fact, CDots surface is functionalized with some (−OH and −COOH groups) which enables the strong interaction between Ag@CDots and the (−OH group) of PVA, which helps Ag@CDots particles to disperse well in PVA matrix [17,23–31]. This good dispersion behavior brought Ag@CDots nano-particles in contact with each other and created a conductive path, which leads to increasing the conductivity of the nano-composite. This behavior is in agreement with the TEM and SEM observations (Fig. 3).

Fig. 5b presents the dc conductivity of Ag/PVA, CDots/PVA and Ag@CDots/PVA for the comparison. As seen, the conductivity of Ag@CDots/PVA is higher than that in Ag/PVA and CDots/PVA due to the creation of a conductive path of Ag@CDots in the matrix of PVA polymer and their synergetic effect, too.

Fig. 6 a presents the dielectric properties of Ag@CDots/PVA nano-composite as a function of the frequency (1 KHz- 1 MHz). The measuring process was performed in a room temperature. Two behaviors were observed (i) the dependence of the dielectric constant on the frequency, where the dielectric constant declines with increasing the frequency. This behavior is attributed to the polarization phenomena, where the time is sufficient enough at low frequency to put the electric dipoles produced in the composite by (CO groups and OH groups and others) in the same direction of the electric field [17,23–31]. This leads to an increase in the polarization and hence increases the dielectric constant. But when the fluctuation of the electric field produced at the higher frequencies the time is not sufficient to put the electric dipoles in the same direction of the electric field. This leads to a decrease in the polarization and hence decreases the dielectric constant [17,23–31]. (ii) The dielectric constant of Ag@CDots/PVA increased with increasing Ag@CDots concentration. The increase in the value of the dielectric constant is attributed to the creation of the network of the microcapacitors in PVA template. These microcapacitors are created by the presence of a PVA thin layer in between two adjacent Ag@CDots nano-particles. This PVA layer produces interface regions between Ag@CDots and PVA molecules. Also, the differences in the conductivity between Ag@CDots and PVA produce a mismatch in conductivity of the nano-composite while Ag@CDots nano-particles is a conductor material and PVA is insulating one. The ultra-high value of the dielectric constant is ascribed to the good dispersion of Ag@CDots in the template of PVA, which leads to creating more and more microcapacitors in the nano-composite. In addition to the strong dipolar interaction between (PVA polymer, −OH group) and the carbonyl (CO) group exists on the CDots surface [24,25,27,28]. So, the electrical charges accumulate at all the interfaces of these microcapacitors, producing the ultra-high value of the dielectric constant. Also, the synergetic effect of both (CDots and Ag) on the dielectric constant was clearly observed for Ag@CDots/PVA nano-composite films, (Fig. 6b). The dielectric value of the Ag@CDots/PVA is higher than that in Ag/PVA and CDots/PVA. This is may be due to the synergetic effect of both CDots and Ag, creates more and more microcapacitors in PVA template than the other nanocomposites. This behavior is in agreement with both TEM and SEM observations (Fig. 3)

Fig. 7 a shows the dielectric loss values of Ag@CDots/PVA films as a function of frequency. Two behaviors were observed from the first sight to the figure. (i) The dependence of the dielectric loss on the frequency. This behavior can be illustrated on the basis of the model of the interfacial polarization or (the model of Maxwell–Wagner), which illustrates the space charge polarization produced in the inhomogeneous structure of the materials [39]. The insulator PVA warped the Ag@CDots particles and isolated it, so a localized accumulation of charges produced under the action of electric field so an interfacial polarization produced in the lower frequencies, so the dielectric loss increases. On the contrary, a very small amount of interfacial polarization produced due to rapid fluctuation of the field at the high frequency region, thus decreasing the dielectric loss [17,23–31]. (ii) The increase in the dielectric loss value occurs as long as Ag@CDots concentration increases. This is due to the growth of Ag@CDots concentration within the matrix of the nano-composite, which leads to formation of a conductive path of Ag@CDots particles and produces a charge transfer complex within PVA chains, so increasing the conductivity and hence the dielectric loss values, σ = 2πƒ εo ε” [17,23–31]. However, it can be seen that the dielectric loss of the sample (PCQG8) is lower than the (PCQG6) at the higher frequency. This is attributed to the fact that in PVA, as the bond rotates with frequency, the presence of flexible polar groups with polar bonds cause dielectric α transition. So, the increase in the concentration of Ag@CDots in sample (PCQG8) higher than the sample (PCQG6), produce a large number of interactions between Ag@CDots and PVA (through its OH groups). Thus, there is altered in chemical composition of the polymer repeated unit due to the creation of charge transfer complex (CTCs) within the chains of PVA, which in turn makes the PVA chains more rigid. So, at high frequency, periodic field reflexion is so fast that there is no excess ion diffusion in the direction of electric field [17,23–31]. So, the polarization due to charge accumulation (interfacial polarization) decreases, leading to the decrease in the value of loss factor of sample (PCQG8).

Fig. 7b shows the tangent loss of Ag@CDots/PVA versus the frequency. As seen, the tan(δ) reduces with the growth of the frequency, this due to the dependence of the tan(δ) on the dielectric loss, which depends on the frequency. Also, the value of tan(δ) increases with increasing Ag@CDots concentration in PVA template. This is owing to the conducting network of Ag@CDots that leads to production of a great leakage current in the matrix of the composite [27,31,39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: EMI shielding studies of Ag@CDots/PVA nano-composites
ce:section.ce:para:
It is well known that the ability of materials to absorb or reflect EM radiation at high frequency range is the electromagnetic interference shielding or (EMI). In fact, shielding of the radiation especially in the X-band (
f = 8 − 12
GHz) is an urgent requirement for commercial, (weather monitoring, satellite communication, and others), and military purposes [40]. In fact, the low level of shielding is 10–30 dB, practical shielding level is 30–60 dB, a high shielding level is 60–90 dB and exceptional shielding level is 90–120 dB. Basically, the total shielding effectiveness (SET) equals the total summation of the shielding effectiveness due to absorption (SEA), reflection (SER), and multiple internal reflections (SEM). Indeed, SET is stated as the following equation,
S
E T
= S
E R
+S
E A
+S
E M

In fact, when SET ≥15 dB, the contribution due to multiple internal reflections (SEM) is insignificant and become negligible. Thus, SET is,
S
E T
= S
E R
+S
E A

So, the shielding effectiveness due to reflection (SER) and absorption (SEA) and the total shielding effectiveness (SET) are stated from the relations;
R =
S11
2
T =
S21
2
S
E R
= 10log (
1 1-R
) = 10log (
1
S11
2
)
S
E A
= 10log (
1-R T
) = 10log (
1-
S11
2
S21
2
)

It is also well known that for reflecting the electromagnetic radiations, the shielding materials should have mobile charge carriers. On the other hand, for absorbing these radiations the shielding materials should have high dielectric constant value (electric dipoles) and/or high value of the permeability (μ) (magnetic dipoles) [41]. Fig. 8 , shows the shielding effectiveness of nano-composite (Ag@CDots/PVA) films in the (X-band,
f = 8 − 12
GHz range). It is clear that SET value of Ag@CDots/PVA nano-composite increases with the growth of Ag@CDots concentration and the maximum SET value of 56 dB was observed at Ag@CDots =8 wt.%, (Fig. 8a). SET mechanism can be described in (Fig. 8b) as follow; (i) The existence of the Ag@CDots nanoparticles creates a high conductive surface in Ag@CDots/PVA films, which produces a large number of free electrons, hence creating EM waves reflection. Moreover, Ag@CDots with large electron density induce currents in the Ag@CDots/PVA films, which help ohmic losses to create a drop in EM waves energy inside Ag@CDots/PVA films [17,23–31]. (ii) The produced interfacial polarization and the film structure are contributing in the scattering of the propagated waves, which create a repeatedly internal reflection and wave absorption. (iii) The inserting of Ag@CDots into PVA and PVA coating Ag@CDots will create huge interfacial regions, which improve the impedance match thanks to the high dielectric properties of PVA also create the polarizations and microcapacitor structures at Ag@CDots/PVA interfaces. This buildings of microcapacitor may attenuate the electromagnetic waves [17,23–31]. Hence, SET of the nano-composite is effectively improved. In fact, a part of the incident EM waves on Ag@CDots/PVA film is reflected at the surface, and the remaining part of EM waves enters inside the film, (Fig. 8b). A part of EM waves was absorbed by Ag@CDots nano-particles and PVA chains. The passing waves will reach to Ag@CDots nano-particles near the surface of the film and divide into transmitted part, absorption part and the other part will be reflected by Ag@CDots nano-particles producing an internal reflection between Ag@CDots and the internal surface of the film. The internal reflected waves will be reflected and absorbed repeatedly by the Ag@CDots in the films, and finally vanish. This operation can be repeated more than one time inside the film due to thousands of Ag@CDots nano-particles. Thus, few waves can penetrate the film.

Fig. 8c shows SET of CDots/PVA, Ag/PVA and Ag@CDots/PVA. As seen,Ag@CDots/PVA has a high SET value higher than both PVA/CDots and PVA/Ag. This is attributed to the synergetic effect of both Ag and CDots nano-particles, where the shielding effectiveness of Ag@CDots/PVA comes from the contribution of both EM reflection and absorption. On the other hand, the shielding effectiveness of CDots/PVA results from EM absorption and the shielding effectiveness of Ag/PVA is due to EM reflection. However, SET value of CDots/PVA is higher than that of Ag/PVA; this is attributed to the higher absorbing radicals in CDots/PVA like the higher dielectric constants (electric dipoles) than that of Ag/PVA and also CDots/PVA has a functional (−OH/−COOH) on the surface of CDots which increases the absorption of EM waves.

A close view on the absorption (SEA) and reflection (SER) of electromagnetic wave, (Fig. 9 a, b), EMI shielding effectiveness mechanism can be studied. It is evident that the reflection and absorption are equally contributed in the mechanism of the shielding of the Ag@CDots/PVA nano-composite. The percentage contribution of EM wave absorption and reflection are evaluated quantitatively at 8 GHz. The % values of absorption and reflection are 47.7 and 52.3 % respectively. Ag@CDots/PVA films show reflection about 52.3 %, while SER is 29.2 dB. Also, Ag@CDots/PVA films show absorption about 47.7 %, while SEA is 26.6 dB.

As seen, EM reflection value is high due to the higher conductivity of (Ag@CDots/PVA) films. The impact of the conductivity on the shielding properties was observed in many researches [40,41]. The presence of more surface charges is responsible for high reflection value behaviour [41]. This high reflection value is due to high/good dispersion of Ag@CDots nano-particles in PVA template, producing more surface charges and greater conductivity. On the other hand, the absorption contribution of 47.7 % for (Ag@CDots/PVA) films is attributed to the functioning (−OH and −COOH) groups existing on the surface of CDots and Ag decorated CDots nano-particles. Edges of CDots and their surfaces have functional (−OH and −COOH) groups [17,23–31] that are responsible for producing multiple scattering, which is considered as multiple channels for the electromagnetic absorption. Moreover, tunneling of electrons from CDots nano-particles also contributes to EM absorption in (Ag@CDots/PVA) films; see (Fig. 8b). Also, the high SE A value 26.6 dB is ascribed to high/good dispersion of Ag@CDots. Ag@CDots with good dispersion property enables more interaction of electromagnetic radiation with functional (−OH and −COOH) groups on CDots and defects on their surfaces, producing an increase in the absorption.

Also, it can be observed that the SEA of CDots/PVA is higher than that of Ag/PVA (Fig. 9c). This is attributed to the functional (−OH and −COOH) groups on CDots and defects on their surfaces, which produces an increase in the absorption. On the other hand, the SER of Ag/PVA is higher than that of CDots/PVA (Fig. 9d). This is attributed to the higher electrical conductivity of Ag composite than CDots composite, which produces more surface charges and hence higher EM reflection.

Table 1 shows EMI shielding effectiveness of the present system and the previous reported systems based on carbon materials and others. It is worth to mention that the shielding effectiveness value (SE) of the present system Ag@CDots/PVA is larger than that of other systems like Nickel fibers/PES, Ag/PVA, CDots/PVA and Stainless steel fibers/PES. Moreover, the value of (SE) of the present system Ag@CDots/PVA is smaller than the value of SE the other systems like Fe3O4/Carbon nanofiber/PDMS and metal materials like Copper and Nickel. From the table, it can be noticed that, the shielding effectiveness value (SE) higher than 25 dB was obtained at higher concentration of graphene, carbon nano-tubes and metal materials. This higher concentration of fillers produces deterioration/weakening of the mechanical properties of the shielding systems due to the aggregation and agglomeration of the fillers. So, it is a challenge task to obtain a higher shielding effectiveness value (SE) at lower filler concentrations in the matrix of polymer. But in this literature, higher shielding effectiveness value (SE) was obtained at lower concentration of fillers due to the decoration of CDots with Ag (loaded CDots with Ag) which gives a small area/small volume occupied by the filler, higher distribution in matrix of polymer with lower concentration, thus higher electrical and shielding effectiveness value (SET) SE features. Also, the obtained (Ag@CDots/PVA) films in this work have some benefits relative to the other conducting polymer nano-composite, such as their easy/simple fabrication, processability, flexibility, usage of small amounts of raw materials and harmlessness and finally being economic and eco-friendly. Also, it is worth to mention that the shielding effectiveness (SE) value of (Ag@CDots/PVA) nano-composite is still below the shielding effectiveness value (SE) of other nano-composites. From this comparison, it can be suggested that, increasing the filler (Ag@CDots) concentration or doping this nano-composite with materials or compounds having high magnetic properties like (Fe, Fe3O4 or Ni) may improve the shielding effectiveness value (SE) of the produced nano-composite.

In this literature, nano-composite Ag@CDots/PVA films were designed using casting technique for the electromagnetic shielding applications. Precipitation method was used to decorate the microwave synthesized CDots with Ag nano-particles. After that, the obtained Ag@CDots nano-particles were embedded into the PVA template to create (Ag@CDots/PVA) films. Decoration of Ag on CDots surface was confirmed by several techniques. XRD, SEM and TEM analysis confirmed that the presence of Ag@CDots nano-particles in the matrix of PVA polymer. In addition, the crystallinity of the obtained films increases with the growth of Ag@CDots concentration, due to the growth of the number of H-bonding in the composite. SEM analysis shows the fine dispersion of Ag@CDots in the template of the films. The mechanical parameters were enhanced with incorporating Ag@CDots into the template of PVA. Young's modulus and tensile strength confidently increase with the growth of Ag@CDots in the template of composite, due to the growth of packing density. It was recorded that 9.1 ± 0.71 GPa for 8 wt% of Ag@CDots as compared to 0.24 ± 0.22 GPa for fresh PVA. Electrical conductivity of the composite increases with increasing the Ag@CDots concentration and its higher value was 31.95 S/m at 8 wt.% of Ag@CDots nano-particles, due to formation of Ag@CDots conductive paths in the nano-composite. In addition, the values of dielectric constant increase with raising Ag@CDots concentration due to the creation of the microcapacitors in the composite. The obtained results of the electromagnetic shielding show the total shielding effectiveness (SET) value of 56 dB at 8 GHz for Ag@CDots/PVA films, due to the synergetic effect of the two (CDots and Ag) nano-particles. The similar values of SER and SEA confirm that the main mechanism of shielding effectiveness of Ag@CDots/PVA films is the contribution of both reflection and absorption of EM radiation, where the absorption results from the tunneling of electrons from CDots. The obtained results (SET ∼ 56 dB, conductivity = 31.95 S/m) make Ag@CDots/PVA films suitable for commercial exploitation.
Ahmed Gamal El-Shamy: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Investigation, Software, Validation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.porgcoat.2020.105747.
